questions.stem: The paired muscular bundles that encircle the esophagus as it passes through this opening contribute to the antireflux barrier by which mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Compressing the esophagus during inspiration to augment sphincter pressure

choices.label: B
choices.text: Providing the intrinsic resting tone of the smooth-muscle sphincter

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anchoring the esophagus to the sternum

choices.label: D
choices.text: Relaxing to initiate the gastrocolic reflex

choices.label: E
choices.text: Secreting mucus to lubricate the passing bolus

explanation.summary: The crura of the diaphragm surround the esophagus at the hiatus and act as an external ('extrinsic') sphincter, compressing the esophagus especially during inspiration when intra-abdominal pressure rises.
explanation.whyCorrect: The crura of the diaphragm surround the esophagus at the hiatus and act as an external ('extrinsic') sphincter, compressing the esophagus especially during inspiration when intra-abdominal pressure rises.

questions.stem: This illustration and barium study show a pulsion outpouching protruding through an area of posterior hypopharyngeal weakness. Between which structures does it herniate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Between the thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus muscles (Killian triangle)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Between the internal and external anal sphincters

explanation.summary: A Zenker diverticulum is a false (pulsion) diverticulum herniating through Killian triangle, between the thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus fibers, due to impaired upper esophageal sphincter relaxation.
explanation.whyCorrect: A Zenker diverticulum is a false (pulsion) diverticulum herniating through Killian triangle, between the thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus fibers, due to impaired upper esophageal sphincter relaxation.

questions.stem: Compared with the normal esophageal lining shown in the upper field, the lower field demonstrates changes of chronic acid injury. The epithelium involved is normally of which type?

explanation.summary: The normal esophagus is lined by stratified squamous nonkeratinized epithelium; reflux esophagitis produces basal cell hyperplasia, papillary elongation, and intraepithelial inflammation within this epithelium.
explanation.whyCorrect: The normal esophagus is lined by stratified squamous nonkeratinized epithelium; reflux esophagitis produces basal cell hyperplasia, papillary elongation, and intraepithelial inflammation within this epithelium.

questions.stem: This endoscopic view of the distal esophagus shows linear mucosal breaks radiating from the gastroesophageal junction. These findings represent the most common complication of which condition?

explanation.summary: Reflux esophagitis, seen here as linear erosions of the distal esophageal mucosa, is the most common complication of prolonged GERD, resulting from direct acid injury.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reflux esophagitis, seen here as linear erosions of the distal esophageal mucosa, is the most common complication of prolonged GERD, resulting from direct acid injury.

questions.stem: Panel B of this endoscopic composite shows salmon-colored mucosa extending above the gastroesophageal junction, unlike the normal junction in panel A. This appearance is caused by which process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Columnar (intestinal) metaplasia of the distal esophageal mucosa

explanation.summary: Barrett esophagus appears endoscopically as salmon-colored columnar mucosa extending above the GE junction, reflecting intestinal metaplasia driven by chronic acid reflux.
explanation.whyCorrect: Barrett esophagus appears endoscopically as salmon-colored columnar mucosa extending above the GE junction, reflecting intestinal metaplasia driven by chronic acid reflux.

questions.stem: This endoscopic image of the esophagus demonstrates stacked, concentric mucosal rings. This finding is most characteristic of which disorder?

explanation.summary: Concentric rings ('trachealization'), along with furrows and white exudates, are the most characteristic endoscopic finding of eosinophilic esophagitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Concentric rings ('trachealization'), along with furrows and white exudates, are the most characteristic endoscopic finding of eosinophilic esophagitis.

questions.stem: Panel A shows white adherent plaques on erythematous esophageal mucosa and panel B a mucosal brushing with branching filamentous structures. Which organism is responsible?

explanation.summary: Candida esophagitis presents with adherent white/yellow plaques; brushing reveals yeast and pseudohyphae. It is the most common infectious esophagitis in immunocompromised patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Candida esophagitis presents with adherent white/yellow plaques; brushing reveals yeast and pseudohyphae. It is the most common infectious esophagitis in immunocompromised patients.

questions.stem: The right-hand panel, in which the gastroesophageal junction remains in place while the fundus herniates alongside the esophagus, carries a particular risk of which complication?

explanation.summary: In a paraesophageal hiatal hernia the GE junction stays intra-abdominal so reflux is uncommon, but the herniated fundus can twist, producing gastric volvulus with compromised blood flow and necrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In a paraesophageal hiatal hernia the GE junction stays intra-abdominal so reflux is uncommon, but the herniated fundus can twist, producing gastric volvulus with compromised blood flow and necrosis.

questions.stem: This endoscopic image shows a longitudinal mucosal laceration at the gastroesophageal junction in a patient with hematemesis after forceful vomiting. This injury is limited to which layer(s)?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Full thickness of the esophageal wall

explanation.summary: A Mallory-Weiss tear is a longitudinal laceration limited to the mucosa (and submucosa) at the GE junction from a sudden rise in intragastric pressure; it usually resolves with supportive care.
explanation.whyCorrect: A Mallory-Weiss tear is a longitudinal laceration limited to the mucosa (and submucosa) at the GE junction from a sudden rise in intragastric pressure; it usually resolves with supportive care.

questions.stem: This intraoperative image shows a large defect in the esophageal wall following forceful vomiting. In contrast to a Mallory-Weiss tear, this lesion involves which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Full-thickness rupture of the esophageal wall

choices.label: B
choices.text: A fibrotic stricture of the distal esophagus

choices.label: C
choices.text: An outpouching of mucosa and submucosa

choices.label: D
choices.text: A partial mucosal laceration that self-resolves

explanation.summary: Boerhaave syndrome is a spontaneous full-thickness (transmural) rupture of the esophagus from forceful vomiting, allowing leakage into the mediastinum and causing mediastinitis; it is a surgical emergency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Boerhaave syndrome is a spontaneous full-thickness (transmural) rupture of the esophagus from forceful vomiting, allowing leakage into the mediastinum and causing mediastinitis; it is a surgical emergency.

questions.stem: In this parietal cell schematic, one drug class binds and irreversibly inhibits the transporter at the apical (luminal) membrane. Chronic use of that class is associated with malabsorption of which nutrient?

explanation.summary: Proton pump inhibitors irreversibly block the apical H+/K+ ATPase; long-term acid suppression impairs release and absorption of vitamin B12 (and raises osteoporosis risk).
explanation.whyCorrect: Proton pump inhibitors irreversibly block the apical H+/K+ ATPase; long-term acid suppression impairs release and absorption of vitamin B12 (and raises osteoporosis risk).

questions.stem: The muscular thickening highlighted at the gastroesophageal junction in this illustration serves primarily to accomplish which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Secrete bicarbonate to neutralize refluxed acid

choices.label: B
choices.text: Generate the primary peristaltic wave that propels the food bolus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Trigger the swallowing reflex via vagal afferents

choices.label: D
choices.text: Produce intrinsic factor for vitamin B12 absorption

choices.label: E
choices.text: Maintain tonic contraction that prevents retrograde flow of gastric contents

explanation.summary: The image depicts the lower esophageal sphincter, a specialized thickening of circular smooth muscle at the GE junction that maintains resting tone to block retrograde movement of gastric contents; its failure underlies GERD.
explanation.whyCorrect: The image depicts the lower esophageal sphincter, a specialized thickening of circular smooth muscle at the GE junction that maintains resting tone to block retrograde movement of gastric contents; its failure underlies GERD.
